title: Pre-Validation BIN Reconnaissance with Sequential Card Testing
narrative: Fraudster performs systematic BIN range validation before live transaction attempts, visible as clusters of $0.00 or $1.00 authorization checks followed by rapid higher-value attempts. The attacker queries 15-40 sequential card numbers within a single BIN prefix (e.g., 442538XXXXXXXXXX) to identify active accounts, then immediately exploits confirmed cards with $87-$299 transactions. All attempts originate from identical device fingerprint (DeviceInfo hash) and P_emaildomain, typically disposable services like 'guerrillamail.com'. Pattern shows 8-12 minute gap between validation wave and exploitation wave. Card1_txn_count_24h spikes from 0 to 12+ within single session. Transactions clustered in ProductCD='W' (digital goods) with international dist1 values exceeding 5000km from cardholder's addr1.
indicators: $0.00-$1.00 authorization attempts preceding genuine transactions by 8-12 minutes
15-40 sequential card numbers sharing first 8 BIN digits tested within 20-minute window
Identical DeviceInfo hash and P_emaildomain across all attempts in sequence
Card1_txn_count_24h increases from 0 to 12+ during attack window
Exploitation transactions are $87-$299 in ProductCD='W' category
Dist1 values exceed 5000km between transaction location and cardholder addr1
P_emaildomain matches disposable email service pattern (guerrillamail, tempmail, 10minutemail)
summary: An attacker tests 15-40 sequential card numbers from the same BIN prefix with $0-$1 authorization checks, then 8-12 minutes later uses the validated cards for $87-$299 digital goods purchases, all from the same device and disposable email address.
reasoning: The criminal is methodically probing which card numbers in a stolen BIN range are active before immediately exploiting confirmed accounts for high-value digital goods that can be quickly resold, with the geographic mismatch and disposable email revealing the transactions aren't from legitimate cardholders.
indicator_explanations: Tiny test charges probe cards before real fraud hits minutes later
Fraudster systematically tests card numbers from the same bank in rapid succession
All attempts come from one device using one throwaway email account
Card goes from dormant to hyperactive during the testing burst window
Validated cards get hit with mid-range digital goods purchases immediately after
Purchase location is impossibly far from the cardholder's billing address
Attacker uses temporary email service to avoid real identity verification
